MASTER CALENDAR JURY

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar May-25-2017 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES - ATTY. JAMES C. MITCHELL OF THE GILLEON LAW FIRM, TEL. NO. 1-619-702-8623 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. GAYLE L. GOUGH OF GOUGH & HANCOCK LLP, TEL. NO. 1-415-848-8900 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT PG&E;
ATTY. CARRIE S. LIN OF MANION GAYNOR & MANNING LLP, TEL. NO. 1-415-527-2802 APPEAING FOR THE DEENDANT SNELSON CO.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Defendant Snelson Companies, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is granted. Snelson maintains its initial burden of production and demonstrates that plaintiff Juan Perez cannot prove causation based on the declaration of Mr. Kollmeyer. Mr. Kollmeyer explains that the Cocci spores, which cause Valley Fever, are wide-ranging across the southwestern United States and can remain suspended in the air for significant periods of time because of the spores' small size. (Kollmeyer Dec., pars. 3 and 7.) Mr. Kollmeyer further explains that no testing was conducted of the soil where Mr. Perez alleges that the exposure occurred and the origin of the spores that caused Mr. Perez's injury is unknown. (Kollmeyer Dec., par. 16 and 17.) In opposition, Mr. Perez fails to present evidence showing that there were any Cocci spores at the PG&E worksite. Mr. Perez does not show threshold exposure (i.e. product identification causation) within a reasonable medical probability based on expert testimony. Mr. Perez relies on the declaration of Mr. Kapin. Mr. Kapin, however, acknowledges that Cocci spores "can remain airborne for extended periods of time and travel in the wind, sometimes large distances in endemic areas." (Kapin Dec., par. 7.) Mr. Kapin also acknowledges that Mr. Perez performed yardwork and pulled weeds while at home in San Diego. (Kapin Dec., par. 25.) While Mr. Kapin attempts to rule out other causes of Mr. Perez' exposure to Cocci spores resulting in his contracting Valley Fever, Mr. Perez's case remains a stream of conjecture. Snelson's objections to the Kapin declaration as speculative and lacking foundation are sustained. Miranda v. Bomel Const. Co., Inc. (2010) 187 Cal.App.4th 1326 is the controlling case. Miranda stands for the proposition that, due to the nature of transmission of Cocci spores, where a plaintiff has contracted Valley Fever in an endemic area, threshold exposure requires proof by some scientific test that that there were Cocci spores in matter (such as dirt or air) that is attributable to the defendant. This principle fully applies to this case and requires the granting of defendants' summary judgment motions. Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR # 8189 =302/hk

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC COMPANY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Motion For Summary Adjudication On The Complaint Of Plaintiff Juan M Perez - Defendant Pacific Gas and Electric Company's ("PG&E") motion for summary judgment is granted. PG&E maintains its initial burden of production and demonstrates that plaintiff Juan Perez cannot prove causation based on the declaration of Mr. Kollmeyer. Mr. Kollmeyer explains that the Cocci spores, which cause Valley Fever, are wide-ranging across the southwestern United States and can remain suspended in the air for significant periods of time because of the spores' small size. (Kollmeyer Dec., pars. 3 and 7.) Mr. Kollmeyer further explains that no testing was conducted of the soil where Mr. Perez alleges that the exposure occurred and the origin of the spores that caused Mr. Perez's injury is unknown. (Kollmeyer Dec., par. 16 and 17.) In opposition, Mr. Perez fails to present evidence showing that there were any Cocci spores at the PG&E worksite. Mr. Perez does not show threshold exposure (i.e. product identification causation) within a reasonable medical probability based on expert testimony. Mr. Perez relies on the declaration of Mr. Kapin. Mr. Kapin, however, acknowledges that Cocci spores "can remain airborne for extended periods of time and travel in the wind, sometimes large distances in endemic areas." (Kapin Dec., par. 7.) Mr. Kapin also acknowledges that Mr. Perez performed yardwork and pulled weeds while at home in San Diego. (Kapin Dec., par. 25.) While Mr. Kapin attempts to rule out other causes of Mr. Perez' exposure to Cocci spores resulting in his contracting Valley Fever, Mr. Perez's case remains a stream of conjecture. PG&E's objections to the Kapin declaration as speculative and lacking foundation are sustained. Miranda v. Bomel Const. Co., Inc. (2010) 187 Cal.App.4th 1326 is the controlling case. Miranda stands for the proposition that, due to the nature of transmission of Cocci spores, where a plaintiff has contracted Valley Fever in an endemic area, threshold exposure requires proof by some scientific test that that there were Cocci spores in matter (such as dirt or air) that is attributable to the defendant. This principle fully applies to this case and requires the granting of defendants' summary judgment motions. Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR # 8189 =302/hk
